nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reefs below the surge zone, commonly near caves, overhangs, and ledges; recorded across a broad depth range from shallow reef flats down to roughly 200 m.

diet.notes: In the wild has been reported to feed on small crustaceans (shrimps, crabs), worms, and small fishes, hunting primarily at night. In captivity generally accepts a varied meaty diet of mysis, chopped marine meats, and frozen shrimp; newly-introduced individuals sometimes need live saltwater feeder shrimp to initiate feeding. Feeding two to three times daily is commonly advised.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in its mouth

imageCandidates.notes: U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service (Pacific Region), 3000x2090. Large in-situ reef school of bright-red, thin-white-striped squirrelfish with large dark eyes; tack-sharp, vibrant, shows diagnostic coloration and conspecific-grouping behavior perfectly.

Bernard Spragg NZ, 2400x1350. Aquarium shot on blue background showing several correctly-ID'd striped squirrelfish; a couple of soldierfish share the frame, so kept non-recommended for the mixed-species context.
Izuzuki, 640x480. Clear single individual under a ledge, bright red with crisp white horizontal stripes and large eye; lower resolution keeps it as a non-recommended detail/ID shot.

summary: A nocturnal squirrelfish endemic to Hawaii and Johnston Atoll, reaching around 17 cm, with bright red coloration and thin white horizontal stripes. Generally considered hardy, though it tends to prey on small fish and motile invertebrates and is not considered strictly reef-safe.
careNotes: The Striped Squirrelfish is a nocturnal species that generally hides in caves, overhangs, and ledges during the day and becomes active at dusk. It tends to do best with ample rockwork and open swimming space, and a tightly-sealed lid is generally advised since squirrelfishes have been known to jump. Caution is warranted when netting or handling: the species carries sharp opercular and preopercular spines reported as venomous within the Holocentridae family, and its rough scales can easily snag in netting. The Striped Squirrelfish is generally regarded as reef-safe with caution — it has been reported to consume small fish, ornamental shrimp, snails, serpent stars, and small worms, though sessile corals are generally left alone. Multiple individuals can be kept together when introduced simultaneously into a sufficiently large aquarium with multiple hiding spots. Adults typically reach around 15 to 17 cm, and a minimum tank volume of around 400 liters is generally recommended for this active nocturnal predator.
